frameworks.name: reinforcement learning
frameworks.one_line: Go/no-go RL task with active vs passive responses to escape or avoid aversive stimuli

frameworks.name: computational cognitive modeling
frameworks.one_line: Model isolates decision-making biases including an active-response bias parameter

frameworks.name: escape theory of suicide
frameworks.one_line: Suicidal thoughts and behaviors framed as efforts to escape painful emotions

core_question: Do suicidal individuals exhibit a heightened decision-making bias toward active responses when escaping aversive states?
key_assumptions: Suicidal thoughts and behaviors reflect efforts to escape aversive internal states
Active vs passive response bias can be dissociated within an RL framework
A computational model parameter can index escape-related decision bias
manipulations: Response type required: active 'go' vs passive 'no-go'
Outcome contingency: escape vs avoid an aversive stimulus
Group: suicidal participants vs nonsuicidal psychiatric controls
key_findings: Suicidal participants showed higher active-response bias for escape than psychiatric controls
Suicidal participants performed worse when escape required a passive response
The bias parameter accounted for unique variance in group status beyond established suicidality-related constructs
interpretations: Elevated active-escape bias is specific to suicidality rather than general psychopathology
Decision-making biases for escape provide a computational signature of suicidal thoughts and behaviors
